resources.string: Kotlin uses \'val\' for read-only variables and \'var\' for mutable variables. The type is usually inferred automatically, but can be declared explicitly. Prefer \'val\' over \'var\' whenever the value doesn\'t need to change — it makes code safer and more readable.

Kotlin has 8 basic types: Int, Long, Double, Float, Boolean, String, and Char. Types are usually inferred but can be declared explicitly. Collections such as List, MutableList, Set, and Map are also fundamental types.
In Kotlin, \'if\' is an expression — it returns a value, so there is no ternary operator (a ? b : c). \'when\' is a powerful replacement for the switch statement and also works as an expression.
Kotlin strings support templates ($ and ${}), multi-line raw strings, and a rich set of built-in operations for searching, transforming, and comparing text.

Kotlin supports \'for\', \'while\', and \'do-while\' loops. The \'for\' loop works with ranges and collections. Ranges support \'downTo\', \'until\', and \'step\' to control iteration direction and increment.
Ranges represent a sequence of values created with \'..\'. Use \'downTo\', \'until\', and \'step\' to control direction and increment. Ranges work in loops and in \'in\' checks inside conditions.

Functions in Kotlin are declared with \'fun\'. They support default arguments (reducing overloads), named arguments (improving readability), and vararg (variable number of arguments). Single-expression functions can be written with \'=\' syntax.
Lambda functions are anonymous functions that can be assigned to variables or passed as arguments. Syntax: { params -> body }. When there is a single parameter, it can be referenced as \'it\'.

Kotlin collections include immutable and mutable List, Set, and Map. Sets automatically remove duplicates. Maps store key-value pairs. Higher-order functions like filter, map, sum, and forEach make processing easy.

Kotlin\'s type system eliminates null-reference errors at compile time. Non-nullable types cannot hold null. Add \'?\' to a type to make it nullable. Safe call \'?.\', Elvis \'?:\', not-null assertion \'!!\', safe cast \'as?\', and helpers like toIntOrNull() keep code safe.

Classes in Kotlin are final by default. The primary constructor is declared in the class header. A secondary constructor must delegate to the primary one. The \'init\' block runs initialization logic right after the primary constructor.

Classes are final by default — use \'open\' to allow inheritance. Override methods with \'override\'. Interfaces define contracts and can provide default method implementations. A class can implement multiple interfaces.

Kotlin offers purpose-built class types: \'data class\' auto-generates equals/hashCode/toString/copy; \'enum class\' defines a fixed set of constants (with optional properties); \'object\' creates a singleton; \'companion object\' provides static-like members inside a class.

Extension functions and properties let you add new functionality to existing classes — even ones you don\'t own — without inheritance. Extension properties provide computed pseudo-properties; they cannot store state, only expose a getter.

Kotlin follows clear naming rules: variables and functions use camelCase, classes use PascalCase. Boolean functions start with \'is\', \'has\', or \'can\'. Function names should be verbs describing what they do. Good naming makes code self-documenting.
val vs var, type inference, best practices.
Int, Long, Double, Float, Boolean, String, Char.
if as expression, when — no ternary needed.
Default args, named args, vararg, single-expression.
{ params -> body }, it, lambdas as arguments.
List, Set, Map — filter, map, sum, forEach.
data class, enum, object, companion object.
